mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers Australian history including the Great Depression, WWII, postwar prosperity, Aboriginal land rights, and the White Australia policy in B.
Australia and New Zealand's Commonwealth relationships, the League of Nations mandate system, and Papua New Guinea's path to independence are examined in context of imperial decline.
Papua New Guinea's independence in 1975 and the dissolution of the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (A.3) exemplify decolonization in Oceania.
The Pacific theater—Japanese expansion, Allied island-hopping, and the war's aftermath—reshaped the entire region (A.2).
Australia and New Zealand joined ANZUS and SEATO, committed forces to Korea and Vietnam, and navigated superpower rivalry in the Pacific (B.3).
The UN trusteeship system administered Pacific territories and guided the dissolution of the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (A.3).
Large-scale European and Asian immigration transformed Australia's demographics and national identity, central to B.4.
Australian Labor governments and New Zealand's Labour Party built welfare programs (B.2, C.1) that became defining features of both nations' social contracts.
Australian and New Zealand democratic traditions, including the 1975 constitutional crisis and Whitlam's dismissal, illustrate parliamentary democracy under stress (B.2).

mappings.vsiTitle: The Great Depression and The New Deal
mappings.rationaleAI: The Great Depression devastated Australia and New Zealand's commodity-dependent economies, shaping the political upheavals described in B.1 and C.1.
The women's movement drove significant social change in Australia, from suffrage through second-wave feminism and workplace reform (B.4).
Australia's vigorous opposition to French nuclear testing in the South Pacific became a defining foreign policy stance (B.3).
Aboriginal-rights struggles, including the landmark 1993 Native Title Act and the Stolen Generations reckoning, are central to B.2 and B.4.
Indigenous peoples of Oceania and the cultural impacts of UN trusteeship administrations (A.3) are illuminated through anthropological perspectives.
Australia's pivot toward Asian and Pacific economic partnerships and domestic deregulation reshaped its place in the global economy (B.2-3).
Indigenous autonomy movements and the evolution of Australian national identity—from British dominion to multicultural republic debates—are explored here.
Japan's imperial expansion and wartime conquests in the Pacific fundamentally altered Oceania's political landscape (A.2).
